Program data illustrates that project staff perceived a lack of funding as one of the main reasons why CAPC and CPNP projects had to reduce (30% and 28%, respectively) Endnote x or refuse (43% and 38%, respectively) services for target populations. Of the projects that had to refuse services, 69% of CAPC projects and 50% of CPNP projects had wait lists. However, the evaluation could not confirm whether the individuals on these wait-lists were unable to receive support from other programs in their community.
Many internal and external key informants reiterated the findings from the program data, reporting that the static funding of the CAPC and CPNP is a significant challenge affecting the extent to which projects are able to continue providing the same frequency and quality of services to address the increasingly complex needs of their participants. Additionally, some internal and external key informants, as well as data from the annual reporting tools, suggest that the decreased purchasing power of CAPC and CPNP have contributed to situations in which the funded projects are unable to hire additional staff to meet service demands and/or experience high turnover as they are unable to increase staff wages. While projects have demonstrated an ability to support participants in achieving positive changes despite having to reduce or refuse services, some external key informants anticipate that this may change if projects continue to reduce or refuse services as their operating costs continue to escalate. In particular, a few external key informants mentioned having to make difficult decisions about the programming offered in order to accommodate rising operational costs. Concerns about static funding for the CAPC and CPNP and the lack of adjustment for inflation were also noted in the previous evaluation of these programs.

### 5.3	Exploring Alternative Delivery Models
Some external key informants recommended PHAC explore if and how alternative approaches to fund recipient organizations may enhance the ability of the programs to achieve its objectives. There are concerns that the current approach may reduce opportunities to respond to changing needs and may result in inequities in the types of organizations funded. However, there are no significant long-term cost savings for recipient organizations and intermediaries associated with a change in approach. Therefore, any changes in program design should consider multiple factors, above all the overall objectives of the programs.
In addition to static funding, some internal and external key informants also raised concerns around the closed solicitation process, which has resulted in the same organizations receiving funding for more than 20 years while organizations in other communities that would benefit from CAPC and CPNP projects have not had the opportunity to apply for funding. The closed solicitation process was perceived to hinder the extent to which CAPC and CPNP projects were able to reallocate funds in response to changing demographics and needs, creating situations of inequity regarding the types of organizations funded and families served. Additionally, several internal key informants and internal program documents noted the burden of administering over 500 CAPC and CPNP agreements. While the Agency attempted to reduce administrative burden by staggering the renewal process for agreements across three years (clustered by regions), key informants felt that the workload of managing these agreements continued to stretch organizational capacity.
Given the challenges and concerns regarding the current CAPC and CPNP approach to fund recipients, several internal and some external key informants recommended that PHAC explore alternatives that would continue to support prenatal health and early childhood development programming within community-based organizations, while offering the flexibility to respond to emerging needs. Potential alternatives mentioned by key informants included an open and competitive selection process, the use of third-party host agencies similar to the British Columbia (B.C.) coalition model for the CAPC, or transfer payments to the provinces along the lines of the Early Learning and Child Care (ELCC) program offered by Employment and Social Development Canada.
An economic analysis Endnote xi was conducted to examine some of these potential alternative funding approaches further, including estimated cost-savings related to recipient organizations, intermediaries involved in delivery and program participants, as well as factors that would need to be considered before their implementation. Overall, the economic analysis found that a change in funding approaches is unlikely to result in significant long-term cost-savings for recipient organizations and intermediaries involved in delivery. It also found that there would not be significant long-term cost-savings related to future health and social services for children that participated in the CAPC, or pregnant individuals and babies that participated in the CPNP Endnote xii . Consequently, potential cost-savings should not be the main driver for any changes made to the CAPC and CPNP funding approach.
Any changes in funding approach would therefore need to consider other factors, above all the overall objectives of the program. Additionally, changes to the delivery model should recognize the longstanding integration of the CAPC and CPNP in communities across Canada, as well as the complex nature of the programs, including regional, provincial, and territorial variations, requirements, and programs. Moreover, there were concerns that changes may negatively impact regional approaches that are working well to meet the needs of participants as a model that works well in one province may not work in other jurisdictions (e.g., there may not be organizations that can assume the role of a host agency). It was suggested that efforts to explore changes to how the CAPC and CPNP funds recipient organization should begin by determining the key objectives of the programs (e.g., funding projects, knowledge translation, stakeholder relationships). These efforts should also be transparent and, done in consultation with the JMCs and funded projects, and ensure the programs are implemented in the most effective and efficient way possible.
International approaches
A review of federal programs similar to the CAPC and CPNP in Australia, the United States, and the United Kingdom revealed the use of similar alternative approaches as the ones examined through the economic analysis, including a third party model for allocating, administering, and managing funding in support of early childhood development. For instance, through the Communities for Children initiative, the Government of Australia offers a five-year grant to non-governmental organizations that administer funding to community service providers on behalf of the federal government and coordinate community-based early intervention services in 52 disadvantaged communities across Australia. This model has supported an increase in cooperation among service providers and the ability to meet local needs Footnote 21 .

## 6.0 Conclusions and Recommendations
Overall, the CAPC and CPNP complement provincial and territorial programs and fill a gap by providing services to at-risk pregnant individuals, as well as young children and their families. The evaluation provides evidence that the majority of CAPC and CPNP participants are members of PHAC's target populations and that projects are primarily located in communities with socio-economic or school readiness vulnerabilities. The majority of participants experienced positive benefits from attending a CAPC and CPNP project, including gaining knowledge and skills, as well as improving their health behaviours, and health and wellbeing. Additionally, projects were perceived as playing an important role in addressing the ongoing and fundamental needs of their participants to support prenatal health and early childhood development.
However, there are concerns that the needs of program participants are becoming increasingly complex and have been further amplified by the COVID-19 pandemic. PHAC could strengthen communication between the CAPC and CPNP and other programs situated within PHAC's HPCDPB in order to build the capacity of project staff and improve their ability to support CAPC and CPNP participants. Moreover, there may be opportunities to further optimize the reach of the programs to target populations. Information regarding the reach of the programs should inform the renewal of CAPC and CPNP projects in 2023, with the aim to optimize their reach. The JMCs may play a role in supporting PHAC's efforts to optimize program reach by helping to identify communities in greatest need for programming. In order to assume this role, the JMCs must be actively engaged in all provinces.
Furthermore, opportunities were identified for PHAC to assume a greater leadership role in further strengthening the coordination and sharing of knowledge products developed by CAPC and CPNP projects, stakeholders, and other jurisdictions to ensure complementarity, expand reach, and support participants. PHAC has begun to make advances in this area in developing a three-year plan to strategically coordinate knowledge development and through the development of a resource inventory for funded projects and recognizes that continued efforts are required to further support the sharing of resources across projects and jurisdictions.
PHAC has made strides to address issues related to efficiency and a lack of clarity regarding the roles and responsibilities of the three branches involved in the CAPC and CPNP through the development and implementation of a Program Charter, which came into effect in fall 2020. While the CAPC and CPNP model provides a flexible platform for program delivery in order to meet the needs of participants, static funding for the last twenty years has resulted in a situation where projects have 33% less purchasing power when funding is adjusted for inflation. This decrease in purchasing power, coupled with concerns over the programs' closed solicitation process, prompted key informants to recommend that PHAC explore alternative funding approaches for the CAPC and CPNP, namely the B.C. coalition model for the CAPC and ESDC's ELCC program. Key informants acknowledged that any changes to the funding approach should recognize the longstanding integration of the CAPC and CPNP in communities across Canada, as well as the complex nature of the programs, including regional, provincial, and territorial variations to ensure the continued complementarity between jurisdictions.
Findings discussed in this report led to the identification of three recommendations to help ensure that CAPC and CPNP continue to generate positive changes for program participants, meet participant and stakeholder needs, and support PHAC's objectives.
1.	In the context of the upcoming renewal of the CAPC and CPNP, the Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch should review program objectives to ensure they continue to be relevant, and address the needs of target populations. As part of this review, it is recommended that the Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch consider ways to strengthen and improve information sharing amongst stakeholders and partners, and better integrate this as a key activity for funded projects.
Over the past 25 years, the CAPC and CPNP programs have remained relatively stable. Evidence has shown that the needs of at-risk pregnant individuals, children and families are complex. Additionally, data suggests that the CAPC and CPNP may not always be reaching target populations. However, it is possible that the demographic characteristics and needs of at-risk pregnant individuals and families with young children have shifted in the last 25 years and no longer align with PHAC's original target populations and program objectives.
The objectives and reach of the CAPC and CPNP have been longstanding issues for these programs. In 2010, the evaluation of the CAPC recommended a review and identification of the program's core objectives, while the 2015 evaluation of the CAPC and CPNP recommended the exploration of opportunities to optimize program reach. As such, program renewal represents an opportune time to examine program objectives and consider the populations who would benefit most from the CAPC and CPNP. It is also an opportunity to clarify PHAC's role with respect to supporting prenatal nutrition and early childhood development. In undertaking this work, the program should leverage results from the recent environmental scan and other work conducted to understand the reach of the CAPC and CPNP, in order to find ways to optimize program reach and respond most effectively to the needs of target populations.
As part of the review of the CAPC and CPNP objectives, the HPCDP branch should build upon their knowledge development and exchange strategic plan and consider ways to strengthen and enhance PHAC's roles and responsibilities regarding resource development and information sharing. Findings from the evaluation show that CAPC and CPNP projects would benefit from enhanced knowledge sharing and resources, in that they would assist them in addressing the complex needs of participants.
2.	Based on the decision taken with respect to program objectives, the Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch should examine alternative program delivery model(s) that would optimize program reach and best enable the achievement of the established program objectives.
Under the current program delivery model, CAPC and CPNP projects have demonstrated an ability to support participants in achieving positive changes. However, static funding and a closed solicitation process have limited the extent to which the programs and funded projects are able to reallocate funds in response to participants' needs. This may create situations of inequity regarding the types of organizations funded and families served. By considering alternative delivery models, PHAC may discover opportunities to continue to support prenatal health and early childhood development programming within community-based organizations, while offering the flexibility to respond to emerging needs of at-risk populations. Such considerations should also reflect the objectives and target populations of the two programs.
3.	The Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch should examine opportunities to strengthen communication and coordination with the Joint Management Committees on investments related to supporting the health of at-risk pregnant individuals, as well as young children and their families.
While the Joint Management Committees (JMC) are intended to facilitate stronger working relationships between PHAC and provincial and territorial governments, evaluation findings indicate that not all JMCs are currently used to their full potential. Opportunities remain to enhance the engagement of the JMCs in activities and decisions related to CAPC and CPNP programming. In particular, including the JMCs in discussions and decisions around the CAPC and CPNP objectives, identification of target populations and potential alternative delivery models may support broader coordination of priorities and policies between the jurisdictions in an effort to ensure continued complementarity of programming.

## Appendix A – Evaluation Scope and Methodology
This evaluation was carried out by the Public Health Agency of Canada's Office of Audit and Evaluation. The purpose was to examine how the CAPC and CPNP fit within the broader context and work of the provinces and territories, program benefits and how they responded to the needs of participants, as well as the efficiency of program delivery. In the wake of the global pandemic, the evaluation also examined how COVID-19 affected the programming of funded CAPC and CPNP projects, and the needs of participants. The CAPC and CPNP were previously evaluated in 2015-16.
The surveillance activities associated with the CAPC and CPNP (e.g., the Canadian Prenatal Surveillance System, the Canadian Paediatric Surveillance Program) were not included, as they will be examined as part of the Evidence for Health Promotion, Chronic Disease and Injury Prevention Evaluation scheduled for 2021.
The evaluation examined the following themes and questions:
Integration with Provinces and Territories
- For prenatal and early childhood development that receive funding from CAPC and CPNP, what is the proportion of resourcing (financial and in-kind) represented by the federal contribution?
- How do these programs fit within the broader context and work of the provinces and territories?
Responding to the Needs of Participants
- To what extent do program participants and communities benefit from CAPC and CPNP funded programs and activities?
- To what extent have CAPC and CPNP been able to respond adequately to the needs of target populations?
Efficiency of Program Delivery
- To what extent do CAPC and CPNP have the appropriate processes and structures in place to address the needs of target populations?
- What is working well and what are the challenges and barriers?
- Is the current delivery model the right approach to address the needs of target populations?
Data for this engagement was collected using the following methods:
With 50 key informants, including:
- 18 PHAC staff involved with the CAPC and CPNP;
- 10 provincial or territorial representatives;
- 12 external funding recipients;
- 3 partners and stakeholders;
- 6 organizations providing MCH programs or services, but not funded by CAPC or CPNP; and
- 1 representative from another federal government department
Included program reports, research, policies, guidelines, etc. The evaluation also leveraged information collected from other OAE reviews, including the 2015 internal Audit of Maternal and Child Health Programs and the 2018 Audit of the Management of Grants and Contributions at Public Health Agency of Canada.
Comparative Analysis
Reviewed the program delivery model of early childhood development programs in three countries (United States, Australia and United Kingdom).
Examined program performance data collected by the CAPC and CPNP through the CPPMT and approximately 159 annual report tool reports.
Financial Data Review
Examined the financial costs of delivering the CAPC and CPNP. Underway – an economic examination of potential costs of an alternative delivery model including residual impacts on partners, funding recipients and Canadians.
Provincial and Territorial Comparison
Assessment of how the CAPC and CPNP fit within the broader context and work of the provinces, and territories, and highlight any instances of potential overlap, duplication, or complementarity.
Examined the needs of target populations in Canada, including the challenges they face and the value of prenatal and early childhood development programming. An SGBA+ analysis was also conducted to understand differences between segments of the population.
Conducted a qualitative analysis examining the key features, and potential benefits and challenges of the delivery models for ESDC's Early Leaning and Child Care program, the CAPC Coalition Model in B.C., and PHAC's Community Action Fund. Additionally, a quantitative analysis was conducted regarding the costs and savings of the current delivery model, an extension of the B.C. Coalition Model to other provinces and territories, and changing the CAPC and CPNP delivery model to an open and competitive selection process similar to the one implemented for the Community Action Fund.
Limitations and Mitigation Strategies
The following table outlines the limitations encountered during the implementation of the data collection methods selected for this evaluation. Also noted are the mitigation strategies implemented to ensure that evaluation findings could be used with confidence in guiding program planning and decision-making.
External key informant interviews were only possible with a small number of individuals from different stakeholder groups.
Findings from the interviews are not generalizable to the broader stakeholder groups or the Canadian public.
Data from the key informant interviews was triangulated with other sources of data (e.g., documentation, program data) where possible
Not all of the funded projects provide the same performance data (e.g., CPNP projects in Québec, as well as CAPC and CPNP projects in the territories do not complete the CPPMT).
This limits the opportunity for a comprehensive and comparative understanding of the CAPC and CPNP impacts across all provinces.
Interviews were conducted with internal and external key informants located in Québec and the territories in an effort to understand the performance of funded projects. The Northern Reports were also used as a data source for the evaluation.
The impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic (e.g., project closing temporarily, focusing on the response) resulted in delays collecting CPPMT data for 2019-2020.
CPPMT data was only available for one point in time (2017-2018). Therefore, the results could not be compared over time.
Program data were triangulated with other sources of data where possible, including the key informant interviews, which focused on understanding the current impacts, benefits, and challenges of funded projects.
The provincial and territorial program review was compiled through an internet search and is not an exhaustive representation of all available programs. In addition, it did not include programs targeted to specific at-risk populations (e.g., Indigenous children).
The findings from the provincial and territorial review had to be contextualized and corroborated with other data sources.
Interviews were conducted with provincial and territorial representatives to gain a better understanding of the complementarity between the CAPC and CPNP, as well as provincial and territorial programs.

Appendix B – CAPC and CPNP Logic Model
This figure depicts the component of the CAPC and CPNP Logic Model, which is a visual illustration of the program's objectives, activities, outputs and expected outcomes.
The target populations of the program are the following:
- Primary Targets: 1) CAPC- Children 0-6 years and their families facing conditions of risk 2) CPNP-Pregnant women, new mothers and infants facing conditions of risk
- Secondary Target: CAPC and CPNP project staff.
The CAPC and CPNP conduct the following activities: under community-based program investments, the program funds, supports and monitors community based organizations to deliver CAPC and CPNP programming to priority populations across Canada; and under CAPC/CPNP knowledge translation (KT) and capacity building, the program funds, supports and monitors CAPC and CPNP projects engaged in KT and capacity building initiatives, as well as develops identifies and shares relevant KT products developed by MCH, PHAC and external stakeholders with CAPC/CPNP projects.
These activities produce several outputs:
- CAPC and CPNP projects receive funding to provide programming to priority populations;
- Organizations from various sectors collaborate with CAPC and CPNP projects;
- Knowledge translation products for participants and projects; and
- Training and capacity building initiatives for projects.
The immediate outcomes resulting from the outputs are:
- Parents/caregivers and their children facing conditions of risk participate in CAPC and CPNP programs; and
- CAPC and CPNP projects access knowledge translation products, training and capacity-building initiatives.
The immediate outcomes are expected to contribute to the intermediate outcome of:
- Program participants gain resources, knowledge and/or skills;
- Program participants adopt positive health practices; and
- CAPC and CPNP projects apply knowledge and use knowledge translation products to support maternal, child and family health.
The long-term outcomes :
- Program participants have improved health and wellbeing; and
- Program participants have improved protective factors.
The ultimate outcomes are:
- Canadians have improved physical and mental health; and
- Health inequalities are reduced.

## Appendix D – CAPC and CPNP Roles and Responsibilities
The CAPC and CPNP are administered by three branches within PHAC, each responsible for different program activities.
Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch (HPCDPB):
- Centre for Health Promotion (CHP): Through various programs, the CHP addresses determinants of health and facilitates successful movement through life stages. Division of Children and Youth (DCY): Ultimately accountable for the CAPC and CPNP, including program management and governance. Assumes responsibility for a number of activities including policy, planning, knowledge translation, funding (i.e. call for proposals, approvals), and supporting the work of the other branches.
- Division of Children and Youth (DCY): Ultimately accountable for the CAPC and CPNP, including program management and governance. Assumes responsibility for a number of activities including policy, planning, knowledge translation, funding (i.e. call for proposals, approvals), and supporting the work of the other branches.
Health Security Infrastructure Branch (HSIB):
- Regional Operations (RO): Responsible for monitoring and supporting CAPC and CPNP projects, as well as working with regional partners and stakeholders to address gaps, as well as identify tools, resources, and opportunities to leverage support on shared priorities.
Office of the Chief Financial Officer (OCFO):
- Centre for Grants and Contributions (CGC): Responsible for managing the overall approach for delivering the CAPC and CPNP contributions, as well as their financial administration.
